title: Networking
description: Build the network model needed to debug common connectivity and web-service issues.

reviewer.summary: Networking gets easier once you stop treating it as trivia. The interview goal is to reason layer by layer: naming, routing, ports, load balancing, and request flow.
interviewFocus: Can you localize the failing layer quickly?
Do you know when the problem is DNS vs routing vs firewall vs application?
Can you explain HTTP and TLS behavior at a usable level?
Can you troubleshoot in an ordered way instead of guessing?

sections.title: Must Know In Time For Interviews
points: Be clear on DNS, IP addressing, ports, firewalls, routing, and load balancers.
Understand what changes when access by IP works but access by hostname fails.
Know the difference between the service not listening and the network path being blocked.

sections.title: Suggested Crash-Course Sequence
points: Learn TCP-IP basics, then DNS and HTTP, then ports and firewalls.
After that, practice routing and load balancer scenarios.
Finish with structured troubleshooting drills across multiple layers.

sections.title: What Usually Fails People
points: Jumping to application bugs before verifying network path basics.
Confusing DNS failure with TCP connectivity failure.
Using layer terms they do not actually understand.

chapters.title: OSI / TCP-IP Basics
chapters.focus: Know what problem lives at which layer.

chapters.title: IP Addressing & Subnets
chapters.focus: Reason about hosts, ranges, and reachability.

chapters.title: DNS
chapters.focus: Translate names to addresses and debug resolution failures.

chapters.title: HTTP / HTTPS
chapters.focus: Understand request flow, status codes, and TLS basics.

chapters.title: Routing
chapters.focus: Know where packets go and why they do not arrive.

chapters.title: Ports & Firewalls
chapters.focus: Allow intended traffic and block the rest.

chapters.title: Connectivity Debugging
chapters.focus: Use the right tool for the failing layer.

questions.prompt: A service URL fails in the browser, but direct IP access works. Which layer should you inspect first?
questions.explanation: If the IP works but the hostname does not, name resolution is the first suspect until proven otherwise.

questions.prompt: A service listens on port 443 but clients still cannot connect. Which checks make sense next?

choices.label: Verify firewall rules and security groups
Confirm the service is bound to the expected interface
Rename the server hostname to something shorter
Test whether the route and load balancer path are open

questions.explanation: Open application code is not enough. Traffic can still fail because of binding, routing, or firewall policy.
